document.file: ./pdf-files/Synthesis of calcium phosphates containing metal ions and evaluation of their catalytic activity for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.pdf
document.title: póåíÜÉëáë çÑ `~äÅáìã mÜçëéÜ~íÉë `çåí~áåáåÖ jÉí~ä fçåë ~åÇ bî~äì~íáçå çÑ íÜÉáê `~í~äóíáÅ ^Åíáîáíó Ñçê íÜÉ aÉÅçãéçëáíáçå çÑ eóÇêçÖÉå mÉêçñáÇÉ
document.img_preview_path: ./pdf-preview-images/Synthesis of calcium phosphates containing metal ions and evaluation of their catalytic activity for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide.jpeg
document.abstract: Calcium phosphates with metal ions are expected to show interesting characteristics, such as catalytic activity, as well as high ability to adsorb organic substances. We assumed that the organic substances adsorbed on calcium phosphates could be decomposed by treatment with hydrogen peroxide H 2 O 2 if the catalytic activity for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 can be introduced to calcium phosphates. We synthesized hydroxyapatite HAp containing Ni or Co ions, and octacalcium phosphate OCP containing Ni, Co or Mn ions. The HAp containing Co ions and the OCP containing Co or Mn ions showed catalytic activity for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 .

method.heading: Synthesis of HAp containing metal ions
method.text:
Synthesis of HAp containing Ni or Co ions was attempted. Calcium carbonate Nacalai Tesque Inc., Japan was heated up to 1373 K at a rate of 5 Kெmin 1 and kept for 3 h to obtain calcium oxide. This CaO 0.15 mol was added to 250 cm 3 of ultra-pure water, stirred for 12 h with N 2 bubbling, and a suspension of Ca OH 2 in ultra-pure water was obtained. Nickel II acetate tetrahydrate Ni CH 3 COO 2 ெ4H 2 O, Nacalai Tesque Inc. or cobalt II acetate tetrahydrate Co CH 3 COO 2 ெ4H 2 O, Nacalai Tesque Inc. 0.017 mol was added to the suspension, and then a phosphoric acid aqueous solution, which was prepared by mixing 0.10 mol of H 3 PO 4 85 mass!, Nacalai Tesque Inc. with 250 cm 3 of ultra-pure water, was slowly added to this suspension at a rate of about 300 cm 3 ெh 1 . The sample notations and starting compositions are given in Table 1. The pH of the solution was controlled between 10.0 and 10.5 by addition of a NH 4 OH solution at room temperature. N 2 bubbling continued during the stirring. HAp with no addition of metal ions n-HAp was also synthesized.
The sample powders were obtained by filtration and dried at 333 K. The powder was heated at various temperatures at a rate of 5 Kெmin 1 .

method.heading: Synthesis of OCP containing metal ions
method.text: Synthesis of the OCP containing Ni, Co or Mn Ni-OCP, Co-OCP and Mn-OCP was attempted as follows. We added 0.100 mol of H 3 PO 4 85 mass! to 1 dm 3 of ultra-pure water, and stirred. CaCO 3 0.133 mol was then added to a H 3 PO 4 aqueous solution at 333 K. After stirring for 3 h, 0.010 or 0.050 mol of NiSO 4 ெ6H 2 O, CoCl 2 ெ6H 2 O or MnCl 2 ெ4H 2 O Wako Pure Chemicals Ltd. was added to the suspension. These sources of metal ions were selected because of their high solubility. The sample notation and starting compositions are given in Table 2. We expected that the added metal ions might be incorporated in the layer structure of OCP, and the CaP molar ratio was kept at 1.33 and metal ions were added. After addition of metal compounds, the pH was controlled above 9 by addition of a NH 4 OH solution. After stirring for another 3 h, the sample powders were collected by filtration and dried at 333 K. OCP with no addition of metal ions n-OCP was also prepared.

method.heading: Characterization of samples
method.text: The samples were dissolved in a nitric acid solution and their compositions were examined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy ICP, Optima 2000DV, Per-kinElmer Japan Co. . Their thermal properties were examined by thermogravimetry and differential thermal analysis TG-DTA, TG-DTA2020S, MAC Science Co., Ltd. . Their structures were examined by powder X-ray diffraction XRD, RINT2200VPC-LR, Rigaku Co. .

document.full_text:
Hydroxyapatite HAp, Ca 10 PO 4 6 OH 2 is one of the calcium phosphates and an inorganic component of bone. HAp is used as a bone substitute because of its high compatibility to body, 1 and as an adsorbent because of its specific adsorption properties against organic substances. 2 Utilizing the adsorption properties, HAp is expected to be a useful material that cleans the environment. However, HAp itself does not have the ability to decompose the organic substances adsorbed on it. A technique that can decompose the adsorbed substances is required to be able to reuse the HAp as an adsorbent. A number of attempts have been reported in which UV irradiation was used for TiO 2 -HAp composites, 3 ,4 HAp modified with Ti IV 5 and HAp itself. 6 However, in these systems, UV irradiation is essential and the decomposition of the adsorbed substances takes some time. We assumed that if the catalytic activity for the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide H 2 O 2 could be introduced to HAp, the adsorbed organic substances could be easily decomposed by a treatment with H 2 O 2 . As an adsorbent, we also focused on octacalcium phosphate OCP, Ca 8 HPO 4 2 PO 4 4 ெ5H 2 O , which has a similar structure to HAp and a layered structure. 7 Ohta et al. 8 reported that OCP showed adsorption behavior against proteins similar to HAp when its adsorption characteristic was investigated by liquid chromatography.
In this study, we attempted to prepare HAp and OCP containing metal ions, such as Ni, Co and Mn ions to introduce the catalytic activity on HAp and OCP for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 . It has been reported that the zeolite-encapsulated Ni and Co complexes can decompose H 2 O 2 , 9 and MnO 2 is known as famous catalyst for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 . The resultant samples were characterized, and their catalytic activity for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 was examined.
Synthesis of HAp containing Ni or Co ions was attempted. Calcium carbonate Nacalai Tesque Inc., Japan was heated up to 1373 K at a rate of 5 Kெmin 1 and kept for 3 h to obtain calcium oxide. This CaO 0.15 mol was added to 250 cm 3 of ultra-pure water, stirred for 12 h with N 2 bubbling, and a suspension of Ca OH 2 in ultra-pure water was obtained. Nickel II acetate tetrahydrate Ni CH 3 COO 2 ெ4H 2 O, Nacalai Tesque Inc. or cobalt II acetate tetrahydrate Co CH 3 COO 2 ெ4H 2 O, Nacalai Tesque Inc. 0.017 mol was added to the suspension, and then a phosphoric acid aqueous solution, which was prepared by mixing 0.10 mol of H 3 PO 4 85 mass!, Nacalai Tesque Inc. with 250 cm 3 of ultra-pure water, was slowly added to this suspension at a rate of about 300 cm 3 ெh 1 . The sample notations and starting compositions are given in Table 1. The pH of the solution was controlled between 10.0 and 10.5 by addition of a NH 4 OH solution at room temperature. N 2 bubbling continued during the stirring. HAp with no addition of metal ions n-HAp was also synthesized.
The sample powders were obtained by filtration and dried at 333 K. The powder was heated at various temperatures at a rate of 5 Kெmin 1 .
Synthesis of the OCP containing Ni, Co or Mn Ni-OCP, Co-OCP and Mn-OCP was attempted as follows. We added 0.100 mol of H 3 PO 4 85 mass! to 1 dm 3 of ultra-pure water, and stirred. CaCO 3 0.133 mol was then added to a H 3 PO 4 aqueous solution at 333 K. After stirring for 3 h, 0.010 or 0.050 mol of NiSO 4 ெ6H 2 O, CoCl 2 ெ6H 2 O or MnCl 2 ெ4H 2 O Wako Pure Chemicals Ltd. was added to the suspension. These sources of metal ions were selected because of their high solubility. The sample notation and starting compositions are given in Table 2. We expected that the added metal ions might be incorporated in the layer structure of OCP, and the CaP molar ratio was kept at 1.33 and metal ions were added. After addition of metal compounds, the pH was controlled above 9 by addition of a NH 4 OH solution. After stirring for another 3 h, the sample powders were collected by filtration and dried at 333 K. OCP with no addition of metal ions n-OCP was also prepared.
The samples were dissolved in a nitric acid solution and their compositions were examined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy ICP, Optima 2000DV, Per-kinElmer Japan Co. . Their thermal properties were examined by thermogravimetry and differential thermal analysis TG-DTA, TG-DTA2020S, MAC Science Co., Ltd. . Their structures were examined by powder X-ray diffraction XRD, RINT2200VPC-LR, Rigaku Co. .
The catalytic activity for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 was examined by adding the samples to a H 2 O 2 solution. The sample powder 15 mg was added to 30 cm 3 of 0.05! H 2 O 2 solution. The solution was shaken at 293 K. After 24 h, 10 cm 3 of supernatant was titrated with 0.02 molெdm 3 KMnO 4 solution to measure the residual H 2 O 2 concentration. The decomposition rate was calculated from the residual H 2 O 2 concentration. The decomposition rate was defined as 0! for the solution without addition of the samples.
Table 3 shows the compositions of the obtained HAp containing metal ions without calcination, as determined by ICP. When Ni or Co ions were added during the preparation, Ni or Co was detected for the samples. The CaP molar ratio of n-HAp 1.54 was smaller than that of the stoichiometric HAp 1.67 . This indicates that calcium-deficient HAp was obtained even for the n-HAp. The CaP molar ratios of n-HAp 1.54 and Co-HAp 1.57 were almost the same, whereas that of Ni-HAp was smaller 1.36 . Table 4 shows the compositions of the OCP containing metal ions, as determined by ICP. When Ni, Co or Mn ions were added during the preparation, Ni, Co or Mn was detected in the samples. The incorporated amount of metal ions increased with increasing amount of added metal ions. The CaP molar ratio of n-OCP 1.36 was similar to that of the stoichiometric OCP 1.33 . The CaP molar ratios of OCPs have not changed significantly by the addition of metal ions regardless of the kind and amount of the metal ions. Figure 3 shows the XRD patterns of n-OCP and OCPs containing metal ions. All the peaks for all the samples were assigned to OCP PDF 26-1056 . No significant peak shift was detected for any samples.
Table 5 shows the decomposition rate of H 2 O 2 for the samples. Regardless of calcination, n-HAp and Ni-HAp did not show catalytic activity for decomposition of H 2 O 2 , but Co-HAp showed high activity. The activity of Co-HAp was increased by calcination. In the case of OCP, n-OCP and OCPs containing Ni did not show catalytic activity for decomposition of H 2 O 2 , but OCPs containing Co or Mn showed high activity, which seemed to increase with increasing amount of added metal ions.
When Ni or Co ions were added during the synthesis of HAp, HAps containing these ions were obtained. The XRD patterns of these samples showed that the single phase of HAp was obtained when calcination was conducted under 873 K. The lattice parameter of the c-axis of Ni-HAp calcined at 873 K was smaller than that of n-HAp. As the ion size of Ni 2 0.069 nm is smaller than that of Ca 2 0.100 nm , 10 the decrease in the lattice parameter of c-axis is considered reasonable, assuming that Ni ions were incorporated in the crystal structure of HAp. However, the lattice parameter of c-axis of Ni-HAp without calcination was almost the same as that of n-HAp. This may indicate that Ni ions were not incorporated in the crystal structure of HAp before calcination and were incorporated by the calcination. There is a possibility that Ni ions exist as amorphous compounds, such as hydroxide, at low temperature. When the calcination temperature increased up to 1073 K, Ni ions were precipitated as Ca 19 Ni 2 PO 4 14 or NiO. On the other hand, no peak shift was detected for Co-HAp even after calcination at 873 K. The lattice parameter of the c-axis of Co-HAp is expected to be smaller than n-HAp because the ion size of Co 2 0.065 nm 10 is smaller than that of Ca 2 . The added Co 2 ions may exist as amorphous compounds, such as hydroxide. These considerations may also be supported from the results that the CaP molar ratios of n-HAp and Co-HAp were almost the same and that of Ni-HAp was smaller. If metal ions are incorporated in the crystal structure of HAp, they are expected to substitute the Ca 2 of HAp, and this decreases the CaP molar ratio, as in the case of Ni-HAp. However, it is still difficult to clearly describe the states of these ions, such as incorporation in the crystal structure, coprecipitation as amorphous compounds and adsorption on the HAp surface.
When Ni, Co or Mn ions were added during the synthesis of OCP, OCPs containing these ions were obtained. XRD patterns of the samples showed a single phase of OCP. However, it is difficult to deny the coexistence of HAp in these samples because the HAp peaks overlap with OCP peaks. We expected that the added ions might be incorporated in the layer structure of OCP, but it was not clear from the XRD patterns whether these ions were incorporated in their layer structure or crystal structures, as no significant peak shift was detected. Moreover, the CaP molar ratios of OCPs were not changed significantly by the addition of metal ions, regardless of the kind and amount of these ions. If metal ions are incorporated in the crystal structure of OCP, they are expected to substitute the Ca 2 of OCP, and thus decrease the CaP molar ratio. Although these results suggest that metal ions exist as amorphous compounds or adsorb on the surface, it is difficult to clarify the states of these ions even in the case of OCP with added metal ions.
The n-HAp and n-OCP did not show high catalytic activity for decomposition of H 2 O 2 , whereas HAp containing Co ions and OCP containing Co or Mn ions showed high catalytic activity for decomposition of H 2 O 2 . This indicates that the added Co or Mn ions in calcium phosphates are effective for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 . Although it has been reported that the zeolite-encapsulated Ni complex can decompose H 2 O 2 , 9 the HAp and OCP containing Ni ions did not show catalytic activity. This might be due to the state of the Ni ions. In the future study, the relationship between the element state and catalytic activity should be examined.
Calcium phosphates containing Co or Mn ions showed catalytic activity for the decomposition of H 2 O 2 . The addition of metal ions, such as Co and Mn ions, gave the catalytic activity to calcium phosphates. These calcium phosphates may be useful as an adsorbent that can be reused after H 2 O 2 treatment.
